content.text: LiteLLM is a Python library (and optional HTTP proxy) that presents a single
over 100+ LLM providers: OpenAI, Anthropic, Google Vertex, AWS Bedrock, Azure, Cohere, Ollama, and more. You write your inference code once and swap providers by changing a single

attrs.alt: LiteLLM architecture — unified completion() routing to OpenAI, Anthropic, Vertex, Bedrock, and more via an optional proxy server

content.text:
pip install litellm

# Optional: proxy server
pip install 'litellm[proxy]'
package has no heavy dependencies beyond provider SDK shims, which are loaded on demand. The
extra installs a FastAPI server you can deploy as a drop-in OpenAI gateway.
. Set API keys via environment variables.

# Set provider API keys via environment variables
export OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...
export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-...
export GEMINI_API_KEY=...
export AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID=...
export AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=...
export AWS_REGION_NAME=us-east-1

import litellm

# Call OpenAI
response = litellm.completion(
    model='openai/gpt-4o-mini',
    messages=[{'role': 'user', 'content': 'Explain quantum entanglement in one sentence.'}],
)
print(response.choices[0].message.content)

# Switch to Anthropic — same call signature
response = litellm.completion(
    model='anthropic/claude-sonnet-5',
    messages=[{'role': 'user', 'content': 'Explain quantum entanglement in one sentence.'}],
)
print(response.choices[0].message.content)
schema, so existing code that reads
. See the LiteLLM docs for the full list — omitting the prefix defaults to OpenAI.
has the same shape as OpenAI's streaming delta.

import litellm

stream = litellm.completion(
    model='openai/gpt-4o-mini',
    messages=[{'role': 'user', 'content': 'Write a short poem about Python.'}],
    stream=True,
)

for chunk in stream:
    delta = chunk.choices[0].delta.content
    if delta:
        print(delta, end='', flush=True)
print()  # newline at end
is the async equivalent and supports
for fan-out benchmarking or ensemble queries.

import asyncio
import litellm

async def chat(model: str, prompt: str) -> str:
    response = await litellm.acompletion(
        model=model,
        messages=[{'role': 'user', 'content': prompt}],
    )
    return response.choices[0].message.content

async def main():
    # Fan-out the same prompt to three providers concurrently
    results = await asyncio.gather(
        chat('openai/gpt-4o-mini', 'Pros of async Python?'),
        chat('anthropic/claude-haiku-4-5', 'Pros of async Python?'),
        chat('gemini/gemini-2.0-flash', 'Pros of async Python?'),
    )
    for model, text in zip(['gpt-4o-mini', 'claude-haiku', 'gemini-flash'], results):
        print(f'--- {model} ---')
        print(text)

asyncio.run(main())
This pattern is useful for side-by-side quality comparisons or implementing majority-vote ensembles.
across a pool of models. Models with the same
alias are treated as replicas; fallback targets are tried if the primary alias is exhausted.
Randomly pick a replica — simple, works well for identical capacity
Route to the replica with the fewest outstanding requests
Track EWMA latency and prefer the fastest replica
Balance by token budget consumed, avoids hot shards
LiteLLM bundles a cost database (price-per-token for all supported models) so you can gate or audit spend without a third-party service.

from litellm import completion
import litellm

# Set a global spend limit (USD)
litellm.max_budget = 0.10   # $0.10 cap

# Or check token cost after a call
response = completion(
    model='openai/gpt-4o-mini',
    messages=[{'role': 'user', 'content': 'Hello!'}],
)
cost = litellm.completion_cost(completion_response=response)
print(f'This call cost ${cost:.6f}')
is set and a call would exceed it, a
is raised before the HTTP request is sent. Pair with a
to automatically downgrade to a cheaper model when the budget cap is near.
The proxy runs as a standalone HTTP server with an OpenAI-compatible
endpoint. Every existing OpenAI SDK client — Python, Node.js, curl — can point at it with no code changes.

# 1. Create a config.yaml
cat > litellm_config.yaml << 'EOF'
model_list:
  - model_name: gpt-4o-mini
    litellm_params:
      model: openai/gpt-4o-mini
      api_key: os.environ/OPENAI_API_KEY
  - model_name: claude-sonnet
    litellm_params:
      model: anthropic/claude-sonnet-5
      api_key: os.environ/ANTHROPIC_API_KEY
general_settings:
  master_key: my-proxy-key   # clients must send Authorization: Bearer my-proxy-key
EOF

# 2. Start the proxy
litellm --config litellm_config.yaml --port 4000
Calling the Proxy via the OpenAI SDK

# Any OpenAI client can now point at the proxy
from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI(
    base_url='http://localhost:4000',
    api_key='my-proxy-key',
)

response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model='claude-sonnet',   # maps to anthropic/claude-sonnet-5 via config
    messages=[{'role': 'user', 'content': 'Explain gradient descent.'}],
)
print(response.choices[0].message.content)
The proxy is useful for centralising credential management (no provider keys in app code), enforcing rate limits, and adding observability across a team.
The same abstraction applies to embedding models.

import litellm

# Embed with OpenAI
response = litellm.embedding(
    model='openai/text-embedding-3-small',
    input=['The cat sat on the mat', 'Neural networks learn representations'],
)
vecs = [item['embedding'] for item in response.data]
print(f'{len(vecs)} vectors of dimension {len(vecs[0])}')

# Switch to Cohere — same call
response = litellm.embedding(
    model='cohere/embed-english-v3.0',
    input=['The cat sat on the mat'],
)
LiteLLM ships callbacks for Langfuse, LangSmith, Helicone, Datadog, and others. Set
to a list of target names;

import litellm

# Route logs to Langfuse
litellm.success_callback = ['langfuse']
litellm.failure_callback = ['langfuse']

import os
os.environ['LANGFUSE_PUBLIC_KEY'] = 'pk-lf-...'
os.environ['LANGFUSE_SECRET_KEY'] = 'sk-lf-...'

response = litellm.completion(
    model='openai/gpt-4o-mini',
    messages=[{'role': 'user', 'content': 'Hello'}],
    metadata={'generation_name': 'greeting', 'user_id': 'usr_42'},
)
dict is forwarded to the observability backend as trace attributes, letting you group calls by user, session, or feature.

LiteLLM normalises the tool-call protocol across providers. You define tools in the OpenAI function schema; LiteLLM translates them to Anthropic's
for providers that do not support it) rather than raising a
Tool call IDs differ by provider: OpenAI uses
. LiteLLM normalises both to a consistent
override. This makes it easy to develop against open-weight models before switching to hosted providers.

import litellm

# Point at a local Ollama instance
response = litellm.completion(
    model='ollama/llama3.2',
    messages=[{'role': 'user', 'content': 'List three Python best practices.'}],
    api_base='http://localhost:11434',
)
print(response.choices[0].message.content)

# LM Studio (OpenAI-compatible local server)
response = litellm.completion(
    model='openai/mistral-7b-instruct',
    messages=[{'role': 'user', 'content': 'Hello!'}],
    api_base='http://localhost:1234/v1',
    api_key='not-needed',   # local server ignores it
)
prefixes for Ollama. For any OpenAI-compatible local server (LM Studio, vLLM, text-generation-webui), use the
Vision models accept image URLs or base64-encoded images in the
array. LiteLLM normalises the schema for both OpenAI and Anthropic Vision.
LiteLLM abstracts 100+ LLM providers behind one
Provider selection is a single string:
adds load balancing, fallback chains, and automatic retry across a model pool.
The LiteLLM proxy provides an OpenAI-drop-in HTTP server for centralised credential management and team-wide gateway deployment.
Observability callbacks integrate with Langfuse, LangSmith, Helicone, and others at zero library cost.
